message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 37 (1 todo)
Recent changes: Added assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy helper called at start of claim(); throws when damage entries of a type exceed insured count of that type.

Refactor while keeping tests green. Evaluate naming, duplication, APP. The claim function now has validation guards plus payout logic. The last test is negative damage amount. Don't add that now. Document. Verify green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 37 tests pass (1 todo). Now I'll work through the Refactor phase systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The recent change added `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy`. Let me evaluate naming across the validation guards and payout logic.

**Naming Evaluation**:

- `claim` — clearly reveals intent (processes an insurance claim). Keep.
- `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy` — clear: asserts damage counts don't exceed policy coverage. Keep.
- `assertKnownItemTypes` — consistent `assert*` naming. Keep.
- `insuredItemFor` — finds the insured item matching a damage. This is also a validation guard (throws if not found), but the name reads as a lookup. Acceptable; the throwing behavior is a natural part of "the insured item for this damage." Keep.
- `payoutForDamage`, `reimbursementRateFor`, `insuranceSumFor`, `fullCapFor` — all `*For` naming, consistent and intent-revealing. Keep.

**Naming Decision**: All names clearly reveal intent and follow consistent `assert*` / `*For` conventions. No rename needed.

## Step 2: Identify the strongest refactoring target — duplication (Rule 3)

The two validation guards `assertKnownItemTypes` and `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy` both manually iterate with a `for` loop. But more importantly, `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy` has a real inefficiency and conceptual duplication: it iterates **every** damage and, for each one, re-filters the entire damages array and the entire policy. For a type appearing N times, the same check is performed N times redundantly.

Let me calculate APP mass for the current `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy`.

**Current Code Mass** (`assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy`):

```
const assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy = (policy, incident): void => {
  for (const damage of incident.damages) {
    const insuredCount = policy.filter((item) => item.type === damage.itemType).length;
    const damageCount = incident.damages.filter((d) => d.itemType === damage.itemType).length;
    if (damageCount > insuredCount) {
      throw new Error(`More ${damage.itemType} damages than insured: ${damageCount} > ${insuredCount}`);
    }
  }
};
```

Component count:
- Constants: 0 string literals in template... template `More ${...}` = 1 constant
- Bindings: `policy`, `incident`, `damage`, `insuredCount`, `item`, `damageCount`, `d` = 7
- Invocations: `for...of` body invocations: `filter` (x2), `.length` (treated as access, x2 → count as invocation 2 each), `incident.damages` access, `throw new Error` = roughly `filter`(2) + `length`(2) + `Error`(1) = 5 invocations
- Conditionals: `if`, plus two `=== ` comparisons inside filters, plus `>` = 4 conditionals
- Loops: `for...of` (1) + two `filter` (2) = 3 loops
- Assignments: 2 (`insuredCount =`, `damageCount =` are bindings, not mutations) → 0 reassignments

The dominant cost is **3 loops** (mass 15) and the redundancy: this runs the inner filters once per damage entry rather than once per type.

I'll refactor to compute the counts once per type using a grouping helper that already exists conceptually in `quote` (counting items by type). This reduces both redundant iteration and the loop count.

Let me apply the refactoring.

content.text:
All 37 tests still green. Let me reconsider the refactoring critically against Rule 2 (Reveals Intent), which trumps APP.

The new version introduces a `.map((damage) => ({ type: damage.itemType }))` adapter to reuse `countByType`. This is slightly awkward — it constructs throwaway objects just to satisfy a shared helper's shape. That hurts clarity for marginal benefit. Let me make `countByType` generic over a key extractor so both call sites read naturally, improving intent without the adapter hack.

content.text: All 37 tests remain green. Let me run the build to confirm no type errors.

No build script; vitest uses esbuild and would surface type-shape issues at runtime. The tests pass cleanly. The refactoring is complete.

## Refactor Phase Summary

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (First Priority)
All function names clearly reveal intent and follow consistent conventions (`assert*` for guards, `*For`/`*By*` for computations). No renames needed.

### Step 2: Refactoring Applied — Rule 3 (No Duplication) + Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)

I refactored `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy`. The original iterated over **every damage entry** and, for each, re-filtered both the full `policy` array and the full `damages` array — performing the same per-type count repeatedly (redundant conceptual duplication and O(n²) work).

The refactor extracts a reusable `countByKey<T>` helper that tallies counts per type in a single pass, then iterates the resulting map once per distinct type.

### APP Mass Comparison (`assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy` body)

Before:
- Loops: 3 (`for...of` + 2 `filter` re-run on every iteration) = 15
- Conditionals: 4 (`if` + `>` + 2 filter predicates)
- Invocations: filter x2, length x2, Error
- Approx total mass: ~33

After (helper + guard combined, but each runs each input once):
- Loops: `countByKey` reduce (shared, 1) + guard `for...of` (1) = 2 distinct loops, no per-iteration re-filtering = 10
- Conditionals: `if` + `>` + 2 `?? 0` nullish guards
- Invocations: `set`/`get`, `keyOf`
- Approx total mass: ~28, and the redundant repeated filtering is eliminated (real algorithmic improvement from O(damages × (policy + damages)) to O(policy + damages))

The mass is lower and, more significantly, the duplicated per-iteration counting work is gone.

### Rule 2 Check (Clarity trumps APP)
My first attempt reused a `{ type: string }` helper that forced a `.map(... => ({ type: ... }))` adapter at the damage call site — clever but obscured intent. I revised to a generic `countByKey<T>(items, keyOf)` so both call sites read declaratively (`countByKey(policy, item => item.type)` and `countByKey(damages, d => d.itemType)`), restoring clarity without the throwaway-object hack.

### Tests
All 37 tests passing, 1 todo (the negative-damage-amount test, intentionally left for the next Red phase as instructed).

### Files
- Implementation: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- Tests: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
**Refactoring**: Extracted generic `countByKey` helper; rewrote `assertDamageCountsWithinPolicy` to count once per type instead of re-filtering per damage entry (removed duplication + redundant iteration).
**Mass Change**: ~33 → ~28 in the guard, plus elimination of O(n²) repeated filtering.
**Tests**: All 37 passing (1 todo) ✅

Proceeding to the next test (negative damage amount) is the next step in the cycle.
